anchors.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 6 Related Work
anchors.text:
## 6 Related Work

Transformer. Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) has led to a series of breakthroughs in various domains (Devlin et al., 2019; Velickovic et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2019; Parmar et al., 2018; Ramachandran et al., 2019). Liu et al. (2020a) show that compared to other architectures, removing the warmup phase is more damaging for Transformers, especially Post-LN. Similarly, it has been found that the original Transformer (referred to as Post-LN) is less robust than its Pre-LN variant (Baevski and Auli, 2019; Nguyen and Salazar, 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Our studies go beyond the existing literature on gradient vanishing (Xiong et al., 2019) and identify an essential factor influencing Transformer training greatly.

Deep Network Initialization. It has been observed that deeper networks can lead to better performance. For example, Dong et al. (2020) find that the network depth players a similar role with the sample number in numerical ODE solvers, which hinders the system from getting more precise results. Many attempts have been made to clear obstacles for training deep networks, including various initialization methods. Based on the independence among initialized parameters, one method is derived and found to be useful to handle the gradient vanishing (Glorot and Bengio, 2010). Similar methods are further developed for ReLU networks (He et al., 2015). He et al. (2016) find that deep network training is still hard even after addressing the gradient vanishing issue and propose residual networks. Balduzzi et al. (2017b) identifies the shattered gradient issue and proposes LookLinear initialization.

On the other hand, although it is observed that scaling residual outputs to smaller values helps to stabilize training (Hanin and Rolnick, 2018; Mishkin and Matas, 2015; Zhang et al., 2019a; Bachlechner et al., 2020; Goyal et al., 2017), there is no systematic analysis on what complicates Transformer training or its underlying connection to the dependency on residual branches. Here, we identify that unbalanced gradients are not the direct cause of the Post-LN instability, recognize the amplification effect, and propose a novel adaptive initialization method.
anchors.summary: This section reviews related work on Transformers and deep network initialization, highlighting the contributions of the paper in identifying the amplification effect and proposing Admin as a solution.

anchors.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 7 Conclusion
anchors.text:
## 7 Conclusion

In this paper, we study the difficulties of training Transformers in theoretical and empirical manners. Our study in Section 3 suggests that the gradient vanishing problem is not the root cause of unstable Transformer training. Also, the unbalanced gradient distribution issue is mostly addressed by adaptive optimizers. In Section 4, we reveal the root cause of the instability to be the strong dependency on residual branches, which amplifies the fluctuation caused by parameter changes and destabilizes model training. In light of our analysis, we propose Admin, an adaptive initialization method to stabilize Transformers training. It controls the dependency at the beginning of training and maintains the flexibility to capture those dependencies once training stabilizes. Extensive experiments verify our intuitions and show that, without introducing additional hyper-parameters, Admin achieves more stable training, faster convergence, and better performance.

Our work opens up new possibilities to not only further push the state-of-the-art but understand deep network training better. It leads to many interesting future works, including generalizing Theorem 2 to other models, designing new algorithms to automatically adapt deep networks to different training configurations, upgrading the Transformer architecture, and applying our proposed Admin to conduct training in a larger scale.
anchors.summary: The conclusion summarizes the findings on the instability of Transformer training and the effectiveness of Admin in stabilizing training and improving performance. It also mentions future directions for research.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > Abstract
anchor_catalog.summary: The paper explores the challenges in training Transformers, identifying an amplification effect due to heavy dependency on residual branches, leading to instability. It proposes Admin, an adaptive initialization method, to stabilize training and improve performance.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 1 Introduction
anchor_catalog.summary: The introduction discusses the effectiveness of Transformers in NLP tasks and the challenges in their training, such as the failure of conventional SGD and the importance of warmup stages. It highlights the differences between Pre-LN and Post-LN Transformers and introduces Admin, an adaptive initialization method to stabilize training.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 2 Preliminaries
anchor_catalog.summary: This section outlines the Transformer architecture, detailing the components like Layer Norm, Feedforward Networks, and Multi-head Attention. It explains the organization of these components in both Pre-LN and Post-LN Transformers.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 3 Unbalanced Gradients
anchor_catalog.summary: The section analyzes the issue of unbalanced gradients, particularly focusing on gradient vanishing in Post-LN Transformers. It establishes that only Post-LN decoders suffer from gradient vanishing and that fixing this issue alone does not stabilize training.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 4 Instability from Amplification Effect
anchor_catalog.summary: This section identifies the amplification effect due to heavy dependency on residual branches as a key factor in the instability of Post-LN Transformers. It introduces Admin, an adaptive initialization method, to control this dependency and stabilize training.
anchor_targets: s training instability is attributed to layer dependency

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 5 Experiments
anchor_catalog.summary: The experiments section evaluates Admin on various datasets, showing improved performance and stability compared to traditional Post-LN and Pre-LN Transformers. It also explores the connection between Admin and warmup phases.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 6 Related Work
anchor_catalog.summary: This section reviews related work on Transformers and deep network initialization, highlighting the contributions of the paper in identifying the amplification effect and proposing Admin as a solution.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > 7 Conclusion
anchor_catalog.summary: The conclusion summarizes the findings on the instability of Transformer training and the effectiveness of Admin in stabilizing training and improving performance. It also mentions future directions for research.

anchor_catalog.section_path: Understanding the Difficulty of Training Transformers > References
anchor_catalog.summary: The references section lists academic papers and resources cited throughout the document, providing context and background for the research presented.
